item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something interesting I thought of. If you flip "Tails, this flip comes up heads.", You make a prediction only on the "Tails" side. So having the coin land on "heads" wouldn't create a contradiction.

Assuming The coin cannot effect on which side it falls (Or it always lands with the Tails side), then the contradiction would have been resolved like in the article.

But if the coin can effect its own flip, it should have landed on "heads" and keep its original property. (assuming the anomaly takes the path of least resistance)

So perhaps we can conclude one of two options:

1. The coin cannot effect its own flip.
2. The coin always lands with the same side up. Regardless on what's on it or the predictions made.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I swear, this photo keeps changing. Asides from that, I love this as a callback to the old days, as it was. Well deserved +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very classic SCP feel, but not totally in a positive way. The Director notes saying "Don't do this again" were so common in the early days. It doesn't feel very clinical and formal like the modern Foundation and has roots in the older, sillier days where researchers could take scips home for personal use and project leads could be demoted to D-Class for doing obviously poorly-thought-out tests. It's hammy, and I can almost appreciate it as a throwback, but it doesn't feel like the Foundation at all. The only reasons it's not a downvote are: the fact that the researcher was janitorial staff five minutes ago; and the neat gimmick of a coin-flip deciding which story you read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*Reads SCP* Oh, that's cute.
*Reads comments* Huh, some interesting theories in here. Maybe I missed something?
*Goes back to article page to re-read* Waaaaaait, something's different. My mind is blown.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought the only difference between the two versions was the picture, but the extra version of the article makes a pretty good article even better. I also think that the short length helps a lot to keep the tongue-in-cheek tone of the article fresh.

Easy +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-4127 is far to unpredictable and far-ranging in its effects to risk SCP-682 gaining an immunity.

item.title: Re: Skeleton Coins!
item.content:encoded: I was under the impression it pulled a CK that, paradoxically, prevented said CK from happening keeping the site in a weird limbo between slightly different timelines.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: just a fun little classical object with a small little twist attached to make it a bit more fun! Love it!! 1+

item.title: Re: Skeleton Coins!
item.content:encoded: Big nice, I’m glad my interpretation was clever!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’m sorry but due to flagrant and fraudulent misrepresentation of my idea I will have to downvote. Changing the coin from always heads to always tails was too far, Hippo. Too, too far.

You'll be hearing from my lawyers shortly, who have just informed me that suing for "damages resulting from unreasonable downvotes" is not actually a thing — so you won't actually be hearing from them at all, which means I'm not even sure how to end this senten

item.title: Re: Skeleton Coins!
item.content:encoded:
As much as I'd like to claim credit for the idea that the coins form a self-referential loop, the initial premise was actually just two alternate universes (every tails was being shunted to one, and every heads was being shunted to another, until the two met at the end).

I find your interpretation that it's a self-referential loop to be amusing and clever enough to support it with a minor phrase on the final entry, though (edited in).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’m sorry but due to flagrant and fraudulent misrepresentation of my idea I will have to downvote. Changing the coin from always heads to always tails was too far, Hippo. Too, too far.

No but seriously you know I love this little guy so much. You had remarkable restraint to not make the neutralization a 682 crossest like I suggested. +1

item.title: Skeleton Coins!
item.content:encoded:
I know you said this wasn't too important, but I'd like to break down the story told in these two possible iterations of the document. There are two immediately noticeable narratives: the quarter narrative and the nickel narrative

Nickel:

  • An anomalous item (nickel) becomes officially designated after Janitor Rodriguez inadvertently discovers its extra effect, Rodriguez gets promoted to researcher in the process
  • An unnamed subject (who we can assume is Rodriguez) tests with the nickel and creates a new anomalous item (previously non-anomalous quarter) in the process
  • Rogers conducts rest of testing and neutralizes the nickel in the process, making the nickel non-anomalous

Quarter:

  • An anomalous item (quarter) becomes officially designated after Janitor Rogers inadvertently discovers its extra effect, Rogers gets promoted to researcher in the process
  • An unnamed subject (who we can assume is Rogers) tests with the quarter and creates a new anomalous item (previously non-anomalous nickel) in the process
  • Rodriguez conducts rest of testing and neutralizes the quarter in the process, making the quarter non-anomalous

At first, this doesn't make sense. The quarter can only be made anomalous when the nickel is designated, and vice versa. However, I propose three possible interpretations:

+ Show interpretations
- Hide interpretations
  • The documents take place in separate universes. This is the easiest explanation, but also the lamest.
  • The quarter mentioned in the nickel narrative isn't necessarily the quarter mentioned in the quarter narrative. This, again, is an easy explanation, but a bit lame as well, unless taken to its logical extreme where a lot more nickels and quarters are being made anomalous, designated, and neutralized.
  • The documents represent a loop. Let's think about this. Part of this is guessing in my part because we don't know who conducted tests 2-5, but here's my reordering of events:
    • Non-anomalous nickel is used in testing of designated quarter, non-anomalous nickel becomes anomalous and is added to LoAO, Researcher Rogers (who I believe is in charge of testing for quarter) is probably demoted, perhaps to a janitor role.
    • Rodriguez becomes researcher in charge of quarter testing, neutralizes quarter in the process and quarter becomes non-anomalous
    • Nickel is discovered to be designation worthy, Rodriguez is promoted in the process from janitor to researcher
    • Now non-anomalous quarter is used in testing of designated nickel, non-anomalous quarter becomes anomalous and is added to LoAO, Researcher Rodriguez (who I believe is in charge of testing for nickel) is probably demoted, perhaps to a janitor role
    • Rogers becomes researcher in charge of nickel testing, neutralizes nickel in the process and nickel becomes non-anomalous
    • Quarter is discovered to be designation worthy, Rogers is promoted in the process from janitor to researcher.

Wash, rinse, repeat. It's an endless loop, nickel to quarter, Rigers to Rodriguez, heads to tails.

Hippo, you mad lad. +1

item.title: heads i win tails you lose
item.content:encoded:
And it's alright, it's comin' along
We gotta get right back to where we started from
Love is good, love can be strong,
We gotta get right back to where started from ~

CREDIT:

  • Images here and here.
  • This idea came about as a consequence of a conversation with Weryllium (SCP-4759 - The Challenger of Donut Taxes) and kinchtheknifeblade (SCP-3420 - Too Many Ghosts) regarding how to make a skip about "a coin that always comes up heads/tails" interesting.
  • Stole the code from Eskobar's excellent SCP-1173.
SPOILERS!
– hide block

The page you're seeing is chosen at random. It's either this or this.

Not super-important, but I thought it made for a fun little twist.

Like the article? Click here for more of The Great Hippo's Skippos!
